title: Consider fixing the minimum required version of Go to a specific toolchain release
body:
<!--
If you are reporting a new issue, make sure that we do not have any duplicates
already open. You can ensure this by searching the issue list for this
repository. If there is a duplicate, please close your issue and add a comment
to the existing issue instead.

If you suspect your issue is a bug, please edit your issue description to
include the BUG REPORT INFORMATION shown below. If you fail to provide this
information within 7 days, we cannot debug your issue and will close it. We
will, however, reopen it if you later provide the information.

---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST
-->

**Description**
Starting with 1.21 Golang changed the meaning of the `go` line in `go.mod` file to now denote the minimum required version of Go to build the project rather than have it only as a suggested version. It also modified what one can supply on the `go` line. Previously, it only served as a hint on the language version. That has changed with 1.21 where it can now consume a specific toolchain release rather than language version and in fact projects are now motivated to do just that for reproducible builds.
While having simply just the language version specified on the `go` line may seem like a working solution now, it seems like specifying a required language only will crumble once this project decides to bump the version to 1.22 while some platforms still being on 1.21:

```
$ go mod vendor
go: go.mod requires go >= 1.22 (running go 1.21.4; GOTOOLCHAIN=local)
```

In fact, the `GOTOOLCHAIN` env variable mechanism was created specifically for the reason of projects' development moving faster than all supported platforms can adapt and bundle a given toolchain version. In other words, let's say one uses Fedora 39 as a development machine which still doesn't ship with 1.22 but is still a supported platform. The intended (intended by Golang) way to go about this is to invoke the above as

```
$ GOTOOLCHAIN=auto go mod vendor
```
which downloads the necessary toolchain for the job and caches it in `GOMODCACHE` for next use. However, the automatic download can only work if the `go.mod` file specifies a fixed toolchain version, not a language version (as the Go SDK doesn't know which one to download):

```
$ GOTOOLCHAIN=auto go mod vendor
go: downloading go1.22 (linux/amd64)
go: download go1.22 for linux/amd64: toolchain not available
```

However, if I point it to a specific release like 1.22.0

```
$ GOTOOLCHAIN=auto GOSUMDB=sum.golang.org go mod vendor
go: downloading go1.22.0 (linux/amd64)
go: downloading github.com/containers/image/v5 v5.31.0
go: downloading github.com/opencontainers/runtime-spec v1.2.0
go: downloading github.com/containers/storage v1.54.0
go: downloading github.com/opencontainers/go-digest v1.0.0
go: downloading github.com/sirupsen/logrus v1.9.3
...
```


<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**
1. Bump the version of Go to the next minor release -> 1.22
2. run `go mod vendor` on supported platform with a version less than that
3. run 2. with `GOTOOLCHAIN=auto` to let go downloading the necessary toolchain for the job

**Describe the results you expected:**
The project can't be built (in the future) on an older but still supported platform unless a specific toolchain version is required in `go.mod`.

Please consider fixing your required version of Go to something like `1.21.0` and then `1.22.0` when the time comes, because it really will check all the marks you'd expect:
- newer toolchain releases automatically pass the requirement
- older toolchains can download the required toolchain if needed
- build reproducibility per se won't be any different than it is now

Alternatively, you can consider pairing your `go 1.21` line with a `toolchain` keyword which took over the original meaning of the `go` line in that it is suggestive only, so something like:

```
$ cat go.mod
go 1.21
toolchain 1.21.8
```

will work with any 1.21.X toolchain by default, but if one uses `GOTOOLCHAIN=auto`, it'll download .8 release if needed. It would naturally work the same way with 1.22 and like requested it would give older platforms some breating room with regards to the rapid release cadence of Go.

comments.body: A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Addressed in https://github.com/containers/buildah/commit/e2f080554e170db37be78f7013c47ff7248b21c0 which caused the go version string to relate to the 0th toolchain release, closing.
